meta.note: Seed concept graph for Claim Tracker. ~50 core AI/ML concepts with relationships.

concepts.name: Neural Networks
concepts.definition: Computational models inspired by biological neurons, composed of layers of interconnected nodes that learn to transform inputs into outputs.

concepts.name: Backpropagation
concepts.definition: Algorithm for computing gradients through a neural network by applying the chain rule backwards from the loss function.

concepts.name: Gradient Descent
concepts.definition: Optimization algorithm that iteratively updates parameters in the direction that reduces the loss function.

concepts.name: Stochastic Gradient Descent
concepts.definition: Variant of gradient descent that estimates gradients from random mini-batches of data, enabling training on large datasets.

concepts.name: Adam Optimizer
concepts.definition: Adaptive learning rate optimizer combining momentum and RMSProp, widely used as the default optimizer for deep learning.

concepts.name: Convolutional Neural Networks
concepts.definition: Neural networks that use spatial filters (convolutions) to detect local patterns, dominant in image recognition.

concepts.name: Residual Networks (ResNet)
concepts.definition: Architecture using skip connections that allow gradients to flow through very deep networks, enabling 100+ layer training.

concepts.name: Recurrent Neural Networks
concepts.definition: Neural networks with cyclic connections that maintain hidden state across time steps, designed for sequential data.

concepts.name: Long Short-Term Memory
concepts.definition: Gated recurrent architecture that uses cell state and gates to selectively remember or forget information over long sequences.

concepts.name: Attention Mechanism
concepts.definition: Mechanism allowing models to dynamically focus on relevant parts of the input when producing each output element.

concepts.name: Transformers
concepts.definition: Architecture based entirely on self-attention, processing all positions in parallel. Foundation of modern language models.

concepts.name: Self-Attention
concepts.definition: Attention applied within a single sequence, allowing each token to attend to all others. The core operation in transformers.

concepts.name: GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer)
concepts.definition: Autoregressive language model architecture that predicts the next token, pre-trained on large text corpora.

concepts.name: BERT
concepts.definition: Bidirectional transformer trained with masked language modeling, designed for understanding tasks rather than generation.

concepts.name: Diffusion Models
concepts.definition: Generative models that learn to reverse a gradual noising process, producing high-quality images from random noise.

concepts.name: Generative Adversarial Networks
concepts.definition: Framework where a generator and discriminator compete, driving the generator to produce increasingly realistic outputs.

concepts.name: Deep Learning
concepts.definition: Machine learning using neural networks with many layers, enabling hierarchical feature learning from raw data.

concepts.name: Pre-training
concepts.definition: Training a model on a large general dataset before specializing it for a specific task, enabling transfer of learned representations.

concepts.name: Fine-tuning
concepts.definition: Adapting a pre-trained model to a specific task by continuing training on task-specific data with a smaller learning rate.

concepts.name: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
concepts.definition: Fine-tuning a language model on curated instruction-response pairs to make it follow instructions.

concepts.name: RLHF
concepts.definition: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — trains models using human preference signals to align behavior.

concepts.name: Direct Preference Optimization
concepts.definition: Alternative to RLHF that directly optimizes for human preferences without training a separate reward model.

concepts.name: Constitutional AI
concepts.definition: Training approach where AI critiques and revises its own outputs according to a set of principles, reducing reliance on human feedback.

concepts.name: RLAIF
concepts.definition: Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback — uses AI-generated preferences instead of human preferences to train models.

concepts.name: Reward Modeling
concepts.definition: Training a model to predict human preferences between outputs, used as the optimization target in RLHF.

concepts.name: Reward Hacking
concepts.definition: When an RL agent exploits flaws in the reward model to achieve high reward without actually satisfying human intent.

concepts.name: Proximal Policy Optimization
concepts.definition: Policy gradient RL algorithm that constrains updates to prevent destructively large steps, widely used in RLHF.

concepts.name: Reinforcement Learning
concepts.definition: Learning paradigm where an agent learns to make decisions by receiving rewards or penalties from an environment.

concepts.name: Scaling Laws
concepts.definition: Empirical relationships showing that model performance improves predictably with increases in data, compute, and parameters.

concepts.name: Emergent Abilities
concepts.definition: Capabilities that appear suddenly at certain model scales but are absent in smaller models, such as few-shot reasoning.

concepts.name: In-Context Learning
concepts.definition: A model's ability to learn new tasks from examples provided in the prompt, without any parameter updates.

concepts.name: Chain-of-Thought Prompting
concepts.definition: Prompting technique that elicits step-by-step reasoning, dramatically improving performance on math and logic tasks.

concepts.name: Prompt Engineering
concepts.definition: The practice of crafting input text to elicit desired behavior from language models without modifying their weights.

concepts.name: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)
concepts.definition: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that injects trainable low-rank matrices into frozen model layers.

concepts.name: Quantization
concepts.definition: Reducing model precision (e.g., 32-bit to 4-bit) to decrease memory and compute requirements with minimal quality loss.

concepts.name: Knowledge Distillation
concepts.definition: Training a smaller student model to mimic the outputs of a larger teacher model, compressing capability into fewer parameters.

concepts.name: Mixture of Experts (MoE)
concepts.definition: Architecture where only a subset of parameters are activated per input, enabling larger models without proportional compute increase.

concepts.name: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
concepts.definition: Combining language models with external knowledge retrieval to ground responses in real documents and reduce hallucination.

concepts.name: Embeddings
concepts.definition: Dense vector representations of inputs (words, sentences, images) that capture semantic similarity in geometric space.

concepts.name: Hallucination
concepts.definition: When a model generates confident-sounding but factually incorrect or fabricated information.

concepts.name: Sycophancy
concepts.definition: Model tendency to agree with user statements or tell users what they want to hear, even when factually wrong.

concepts.name: Calibration
concepts.definition: How well a model's expressed confidence matches its actual accuracy — a well-calibrated model is right 80% of the time when it says it's 80% sure.

concepts.name: AI Safety
concepts.definition: Research field focused on ensuring advanced AI systems behave as intended and don't cause unintended harm.

concepts.name: Mechanistic Interpretability
concepts.definition: Reverse-engineering the internal computations of neural networks to understand how they process information and make decisions.

concepts.name: Superposition
concepts.definition: The hypothesis that neural networks represent more features than they have dimensions by encoding features as overlapping directions.

concepts.name: Sparse Autoencoders
concepts.definition: Tool for mechanistic interpretability that decomposes model activations into interpretable features by enforcing sparsity.

concepts.name: Multimodal Models
concepts.definition: Models that process and generate across multiple modalities (text, images, audio, video) within a unified architecture.

concepts.name: Vision Transformers (ViT)
concepts.definition: Applying the transformer architecture to image patches instead of text tokens, rivaling CNNs on vision tasks.

concepts.name: CLIP
concepts.definition: Model trained on image-text pairs using contrastive learning, enabling zero-shot image classification and cross-modal retrieval.

concepts.name: Contrastive Learning
concepts.definition: Self-supervised method that learns representations by pulling similar examples together and pushing dissimilar ones apart.

concepts.name: Batch Normalization
concepts.definition: Technique that normalizes layer inputs across a mini-batch, stabilizing and accelerating deep network training.

concepts.name: Dropout
concepts.definition: Regularization technique that randomly deactivates neurons during training, preventing co-adaptation and reducing overfitting.

concepts.name: Transfer Learning
concepts.definition: Using knowledge gained from one task or domain to improve performance on a different but related task.

concepts.name: Foundation Models
concepts.definition: Large models pre-trained on broad data that can be adapted to many downstream tasks, serving as a shared starting point.

concepts.name: Tokenization
concepts.definition: The process of breaking text into subword units (tokens) that serve as the atomic inputs to language models.

concepts.name: Positional Encoding
concepts.definition: Method for injecting sequence position information into transformer models, which otherwise have no inherent notion of order.

concepts.name: Agentic AI
concepts.definition: AI systems that can autonomously plan, use tools, and take actions to accomplish goals over extended interactions.

concepts.name: Tool Use
concepts.definition: Ability of language models to invoke external tools (search, code execution, APIs) to extend their capabilities.
